pmos

Financial Metrics & Unit Economics — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · Cross-Functional Skills. Every product business runs on the same quiet question: do you make more from a customer than it costs to win and serve them? The metrics in this primer answer it. This is the operating dashboard of a product company in plain English, built for product managers. It covers what unit economics are; revenue and recurring revenue; gross margin; contribution margin; customer acquisition cost (CAC); customer lifetime value (LTV); the LTV-to-CAC ratio; the CAC payback period; churn and retention; burn rate and runway; the Rule of 40; what a PM actually needs from all of it; and where to go next. Every term of art is defined on first use, and every acronym is spelled out. Source provenance: curation-consensus across the modern product-metrics canon — Andreessen Horowitz, Stripe, Bessemer Venture Partners, Paddle, Corporate Finance Institute, and the Wikipedia metrics corpus. 17 verified sources.

What unit economics are

Unit economics are the revenue and costs of your business measured one customer at a time, instead of in one big company-wide lump. Pick a single customer. How much money do they bring in over their whole relationship with you? How much did it cost to win them and keep them? Unit economics is just those two numbers, side by side.

Basedash puts it cleanly: unit economics "measure the profitability of a single customer: the revenue they generate over their lifetime (LTV) versus the cost to acquire them (CAC)." LTV is lifetime value and CAC is customer acquisition cost — both are defined in full later in this primer.

The reason this framing matters is that company-level profit can hide a broken business. A company can post growing total revenue while losing money on every single customer. Growth then makes things worse, not better. As the same source warns, unit economics answer "the most fundamental business question — do you make more money from a customer than it costs to acquire and serve them? If the answer is no, growing faster just means losing money faster." That single sentence is why investors and operators look at the unit before they look at the total.

Revenue and recurring revenue

Revenue is the money a business earns from selling its product. For a product company, the most prized kind is recurring revenue — money that comes back on a schedule instead of once. Wikipedia defines it as "revenue that is likely to continue to be generated regularly for a significant period of time," the kind you get from subscriptions and service contracts rather than one-off sales.

Software-as-a-service (SaaS) businesses — companies that rent software by subscription instead of selling it once — track recurring revenue in two units. Monthly recurring revenue (MRR) is the normalized subscription revenue you expect each month. Annual recurring revenue (ARR) is the yearly version, roughly MRR times twelve, used for board and investor conversations. Andreessen Horowitz, the venture-capital firm often shortened to a16z, defines ARR as "a measure of revenue components that are recurring in nature" that "should exclude one-time (non-recurring) fees and professional service fees." The discipline there matters: setup fees and consulting hours are real money, but they are not recurring, so folding them into ARR overstates how durable your revenue really is.

One more revenue lens shows up constantly: average revenue per user (ARPU), sometimes written average revenue per unit. Wikipedia defines it as "the total revenue divided by the number of subscribers." ARPU tells you how much a typical customer is worth per period, and it feeds directly into the lifetime-value and payback math later in this primer.

Gross margin

Revenue is only half the story. The other half is what it costs to deliver the product. Gross margin measures how much of each sales dollar is left after you pay the direct costs of producing what you sold. Those direct costs are called cost of goods sold (COGS) — for software, that is things like hosting, payment processing, and customer support.

Wikipedia gives the formula plainly: gross margin is "the difference between revenue and cost of goods sold (COGS), divided by revenue," or (Revenue − COGS) ÷ Revenue. It also draws a distinction worth keeping straight: "gross profit" is technically an absolute monetary amount, while "gross margin" is a percentage or ratio. Gross profit is a dollar figure; gross margin is a percentage.

Why product managers care: a high gross margin is what makes a software business special. Corporate Finance Institute notes that a higher gross margin ratio means the company retains more profit from each dollar of sales after covering the cost of goods sold — money that can then pay for sales, research, and growth. Strong SaaS businesses often run gross margins of 70 percent or more, which is precisely why so much can be spent winning customers.

Contribution margin

Gross margin looks at the whole business. Contribution margin zooms in on a single product or a single sale. It strips out only the costs that change with each unit sold — the variable costs — and ignores fixed costs like office rent or salaried staff. Wikipedia defines it as "the selling price per unit minus the variable cost per unit."

The reason both metrics exist is that they answer different questions. Wikipedia explains that contribution "seeks to separate out variable costs... from fixed costs... on the basis of economic analysis of the nature of the expense, whereas gross margin is determined using accounting standards." In other words, gross margin is an accounting view of the whole business; contribution margin is a decision view of one product line.

Shopify makes the practical split concrete. Contribution margin "can also help a business owner decide which product lines to invest in and which to cut," while gross margin "accounts for any costs directly involved in producing and distributing what a business sells, providing an overall sense of potential profitability." For a PM choosing between two features or two plans, contribution margin is usually the sharper tool, because it shows which one actually earns more per sale.

Customer acquisition cost (CAC)

Customer acquisition cost (CAC) is what it costs, on average, to win one new customer. Corporate Finance Institute defines it simply as "the cost related to acquiring a new customer," calculated as Sales and Marketing Expenses ÷ Number of New Customers over the same period.

The most common mistake is undercounting. CAC is not just ad spend. a16z insists CAC "should be the full cost of acquiring users, stated on a per user basis." "Full cost" means the salaries of the sales and marketing team, the software tools they use, events, and content — not only the money handed to ad platforms. A CAC that counts only ads looks flattering and lies.

For a product manager, CAC is the price tag on growth. If a customer costs $400 to acquire, every pricing, onboarding, and retention decision has to be weighed against earning that $400 back. CAC on its own means nothing, though — it only becomes meaningful next to lifetime value, which is the next metric.

Customer lifetime value (LTV)

Customer lifetime value (LTV) is the total profit you expect from a customer across the entire relationship. Wikipedia defines it as "an estimation and prediction of the net profit that a customer contributes to during the entire future relationship with a business." The word "profit" matters: a good LTV uses margin, not raw revenue, so it reflects money you actually keep.

A widely used simple formula ties three metrics together. LTV equals average monthly revenue per customer, times gross margin, divided by the monthly churn rate. Wikipedia's worked example: $100 average monthly spend × 25% margin ÷ 5% monthly churn = $500 LTV. Higher prices and higher margins lift LTV; faster churn crushes it.

That churn term deserves a note, because it sets how long a customer stays. Churn rate is, per Wikipedia, the measure of "the proportion of individuals... customers, or items that leave a defined group during a specified period of time." The neat trick: "a customer lifetime can be calculated as the inverse of that customer's predicted churn rate." A 25 percent annual churn implies a four-year average lifetime, because one divided by 0.25 equals four. Cut churn and you lengthen the lifetime, which directly raises LTV.

The LTV-to-CAC ratio

Put lifetime value over acquisition cost and you get the single most-watched number in product-business economics: the LTV-to-CAC ratio. Paddle describes it as the figure that "determines the long-term value of acquiring a customer." It answers: for every dollar spent winning a customer, how many dollars come back?

The rule of thumb is 3 to 1. Paddle states the benchmark directly: "The benchmark LTV:CAC ratio is 3:1. That means if you pay $4,200 to acquire each customer at an LTV of $12,600, each acquired user is worth 3x what you paid to earn their business." Below roughly 3 to 1, acquisition is too expensive to be healthy. Far above it is not automatically good news — a very high ratio often means you are underspending on growth and leaving customers on the table for competitors to win.

The CAC payback period

The LTV-to-CAC ratio tells you whether acquisition pays off eventually. The CAC payback period tells you how fast. Airtree Ventures defines it as the measure of "how long it takes for you to earn enough gross profit from a customer to pay back the S&M costs of acquiring them," where S&M is sales and marketing.

The formula adjusts for margin: CAC ÷ (Monthly Recurring Revenue × Gross Margin). You divide what you spent by the gross profit the customer throws off each month, which gives the number of months to break even. This metric matters because it is about cash and time, not just eventual return. A customer who repays their CAC in 6 months frees up cash to go win the next one; a 30-month payback ties up cash you may not have.

Benchmarks vary by who you sell to. Airtree's targets for business-to-business companies are under 12 months selling to small and medium enterprises, under 18 months for the mid-market, and under 24 months for enterprise accounts. Bigger customers cost more and take longer to repay, and that can be fine — as long as they also stay longer and spend more.

Churn and retention

Because lifetime value lives or dies on how long customers stay, retention gets its own pair of metrics. The cleanest are net revenue retention (NRR) and gross revenue retention (GRR), both measured on your existing customers over a period, ignoring brand-new ones.

Stripe defines NRR as the metric reflecting "a company's ability to retain and grow revenue from its existing customer base over a given time period," and GRR as the share of recurring revenue retained "excluding any upsell, cross-sell, or expansion revenue." The difference is expansion. GRR counts only what you lost to cancellations and downgrades, so it can never beat 100 percent. NRR adds back the extra money existing customers spend, so it can exceed 100 percent: as Stripe notes, "an NRR over 100% suggests that growth from upsells and expansions exceeds lost revenue."

For a product manager this is gold. NRR above 100 percent means your installed base grows revenue even if you never win another customer — a sign the product gets more valuable the longer people use it. A wide gap between a strong NRR and a weak GRR is a warning: a few expanding accounts may be masking heavy churn underneath.

Burn rate and runway

Unit economics describe whether each customer is profitable. Burn rate and runway describe whether the company stays alive long enough to get there. Wikipedia defines burn rate as "the rate at which a company consumes its cash," usually stated per month.

There are two flavors. Gross burn is total cash spent each month. Net burn is gross burn minus revenue — the amount the bank balance actually drops. Runway is how many months of cash remain: cash in the bank divided by net burn. Wikipedia notes that burn rate, combined with available funds, "provides a time measure to when the next funding event needs to take place." If you hold $1.2 million and burn $100,000 net per month, you have twelve months of runway.

a16z frames the investor lens: it gives monthly cash burn as "cash balance at the beginning of the year minus cash balance end of the year / 12," and watches net burn to judge runway. Why a PM should care: roadmap ambition is bounded by runway. A feature that takes nine months to ship and pays off in month fifteen is irrelevant if the runway runs out in month ten.

The Rule of 40

The last headline metric ties growth and profit into one score: the Rule of 40. Wall Street Prep states it as the rule that "at scale, the combined value of revenue growth rate and profit margin should exceed 40% for healthy SaaS companies." The formula adds two percentages: recurring revenue growth rate plus profit margin, often measured as the EBITDA margin — earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization, as a share of revenue.

The point is balance. A company growing 50 percent a year while losing money can still clear the bar, and so can one growing 10 percent at a healthy 30 percent margin. Wall Street Prep credits venture capitalist Brad Feld with popularizing the metric for evaluating SaaS company health. It became shorthand for "is this company trading growth and profit sensibly?"

The rule keeps evolving. Bessemer Venture Partners introduced a refinement called the Rule of X, which it calls "an adjusted form of Rule of 40 taking composition (and weighting) into account," arguing that for late-stage cloud companies "revenue growth should be weighted 2-3x more than FCF margin when assessing valuation." FCF is free cash flow. The takeaway for a PM: growth and profit are not equally prized — at most stages, durable growth is worth more.

What product managers actually need

You do not need to build these models. You need to read them and know which lever you control. Three habits cover most of the job.

First, connect your decisions to the unit. A pricing change moves ARPU and margin, which move LTV. An onboarding fix lowers churn, which lengthens lifetime, which raises LTV and improves the LTV-to-CAC ratio. A self-serve flow lowers CAC and shortens payback. When you pitch a feature, name the metric it moves.

Second, respect the difference between healthy and vanity numbers. Total revenue can rise while unit economics rot. A flattering CAC may be undercounting cost. A great NRR may be hiding churn. The good metrics are the ones measured per customer and net of the costs you actually pay — that is the whole reason unit economics, in Basedash's words, expose whether "growing faster just means losing money faster."

Third, hold the chain in your head: revenue and margin set how much a customer is worth; CAC sets what they cost; churn sets how long they stay; LTV-to-CAC and payback say whether growth pays for itself; burn and runway say how long you have to prove it; and the Rule of 40 grades the balance of growth and profit overall. Every one of those is a number you can influence from the product.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

These pointers are starting points, not lessons — each is a topic worth its own primer.

  • Pricing and packaging — how price tiers and plan design directly move ARPU, margin, and LTV.
  • Corporate finance and valuation — how the cash flows these metrics describe roll up into what a company is worth, via discounting and the time value of money.
  • Cohort analysis — reading retention and churn by customer cohort rather than as a single blended number, which is how the metrics above are stress-tested.
  • Accounting basics — where revenue, COGS, and EBITDA come from on the income statement, and the rules that govern them.
  • Growth modeling — turning CAC, payback, and burn into a forecast that says how fast you can responsibly spend to grow.

References

  1. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T1)

    Wikipedia defines recurring revenue as "revenue that is likely to continue to be generated regularly for a significant period of time," typically from subscriptions or service contracts such as monthly phone plans that guarantee revenue for the contract duration.

  2. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T1)

    Wikipedia defines average revenue per user (ARPU) as "a measure used primarily by consumer communications, digital media, and networking companies, defined as the total revenue divided by the number of subscribers." Because subscriber counts move daily, analysts divide total revenue for a period by the average number of units in that period.

  3. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T1)

    Wikipedia defines gross margin as "the difference between revenue and cost of goods sold (COGS), divided by revenue," expressed as Gross margin % = (Revenue − COGS) / Revenue × 100%. It notes the distinction that "gross profit" is an absolute monetary amount while "gross margin" is the percentage or ratio.

  4. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T1)

    Wikipedia defines contribution margin as "the selling price per unit minus the variable cost per unit" (C = P − V). It separates contribution from gross margin: a contribution calculation "seeks to separate out variable costs... from fixed costs... on the basis of economic analysis of the nature of the expense, whereas gross margin is determined using accounting standards."

  5. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T1)

    Wikipedia defines customer lifetime value (LTV) as "an estimation and prediction of the net profit that a customer contributes to during the entire future relationship with a business." A common simplified formula is (Average Monthly Revenue per Customer × Gross Margin per Customer) ÷ Monthly Churn Rate — e.g. $100 monthly spend × 25% margin ÷ 5% churn = $500 LTV.

  6. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T1)

    Wikipedia defines churn rate (also called attrition or customer turnover) as the metric measuring "the proportion of individuals... customers, or items that leave a defined group during a specified period of time." Crucially, "a customer lifetime can be calculated as the inverse of that customer's predicted churn rate" — a 25% annual churn implies a 4-year average lifetime (1 ÷ 0.25 = 4).

  7. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T1)

    Wikipedia defines burn rate as "the rate at which a company consumes its cash," typically expressed monthly and used for startups. Combined with available funds, it "provides a time measure to when the next funding event needs to take place" — the practical basis for calculating runway.

  8. basedash.com ·cited· (T2)

    Basedash defines unit economics as the metrics that "measure the profitability of a single customer: the revenue they generate over their lifetime (LTV) versus the cost to acquire them (CAC)." It frames why this matters before scaling: they answer "the most fundamental business question — do you make more money from a customer than it costs to acquire and serve them? If the answer is no, growing faster just means losing money faster."

  9. a16z.com ·cited· (T2)

    Andreessen Horowitz (a16z) defines ARR as "a measure of revenue components that are recurring in nature" that "should exclude one-time (non-recurring) fees and professional service fees," and insists CAC "should be the full cost of acquiring users, stated on a per user basis" while LTV is "the present value of the future net profit from the customer over the duration of the relationship." It gives monthly unit churn as "lost customers/prior month total."

  10. corporatefinanceinstitute.com ·cited· (T2)

    Corporate Finance Institute defines the Gross Margin Ratio as "a profitability ratio that compares the gross margin of a company to its revenue," computed as (Revenue – COGS) / Revenue. A higher ratio indicates the company retains more profit from each sales dollar after covering the cost of goods sold — a signal of better profitability and cost efficiency.

  11. shopify.com ·cited· (T2)

    Shopify frames the practical split: contribution margin "measures variable costs of producing a product or service, such as sales commissions" and "can also help a business owner decide which product lines to invest in and which to cut," while gross margin "accounts for any costs directly involved in producing and distributing what a business sells, providing an overall sense of potential profitability." Contribution margin is per-product; gross margin is whole-business.

  12. corporatefinanceinstitute.com ·cited· (T2)

    CFI defines customer acquisition cost (CAC) as "the cost related to acquiring a new customer," computed as CAC = Sales and Marketing Expenses ÷ Number of New Customers. The metric helps a business evaluate how efficiently it is spending to bring on new customers.

  13. paddle.com ·cited· (T2)

    Paddle defines the LTV:CAC ratio as the figure that "determines the long-term value of acquiring a customer," found by dividing lifetime value by acquisition cost. It gives the rule of thumb: "The benchmark LTV:CAC ratio is 3:1. That means if you pay $4,200 to acquire each customer at an LTV of $12,600, each acquired user is worth 3x what you paid to earn their business."

  14. airtree.vc ·cited· (T2)

    Airtree Ventures defines the CAC payback period as the measure of "how long it takes for you to earn enough gross profit from a customer to pay back the S&M costs of acquiring them," computed as CAC ÷ (Monthly Recurring Revenue × Gross Margin). Its B2B benchmarks: under 12 months selling to SMEs, under 18 to mid-market, under 24 for enterprise accounts.

  15. stripe.com ·cited· (T2)

    Stripe defines net revenue retention (NRR) as the metric reflecting "a company's ability to retain and grow revenue from its existing customer base over a given time period," and gross revenue retention (GRR) as the percentage of recurring revenue retained "excluding any upsell, cross-sell, or expansion revenue." GRR caps at 100% because it only subtracts losses; "an NRR over 100% suggests that growth from upsells and expansions exceeds lost revenue."

  16. wallstreetprep.com ·cited· (T2)

    Wall Street Prep states the Rule of 40 as the rule that "at scale, the combined value of revenue growth rate and profit margin should exceed 40% for healthy SaaS companies," expressed as Recurring Revenue Growth Rate (%) + EBITDA Margin (%). It credits venture capitalist Brad Feld with popularizing the metric for evaluating SaaS company health.

  17. bvp.com ·cited· (T2)

    Bessemer Venture Partners introduces the Rule of X as "an adjusted form of Rule of 40 taking composition (and weighting) into account," arguing that "for late-stage cloud companies, revenue growth should be weighted 2-3x more than FCF margin when assessing valuation." It was created by Byron Deeter and Sam Bondy to "more accurately represent a cloud business's valuation."

pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-14_financial-metrics-and-unit-economics.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
